text.title: typescript-patterns Core Rules
text.summary: Seven testable rules for domain-typed TypeScript code under strict mode.

rule.statement: Use the built-in utility types — Partial, Required, Readonly, Pick, Omit, Record, Awaited, ReturnType, Parameters — over hand-rolled mapped types when the standard form fits.
rule.rationale: Standard utility types are compiler-optimised, universally recognised by reviewers, and inferred faster than custom mapped types. Hand-rolled equivalents add maintenance cost without payoff.

rule.statement: Model success/failure or variant returns as a discriminated union with a literal-string or boolean discriminant — never as a nullable single-shape return.
rule.rationale: Discriminated unions force the caller to handle each variant via the narrowing check, and `switch (x.kind)` becomes exhaustive. Nullable returns are silently ignorable and carry no error information.

rule.statement: Validate every external input (HTTP body, env, queue message, form data) at the boundary with Zod (or equivalent). Derive the static TS type via z.infer; never declare a parallel interface for the same shape.
rule.rationale: A duplicate interface drifts from the schema; tests pass against one and prod fails against the other. z.infer pins the static type to the runtime validator's source of truth.

rule.statement: For any string-typed ID that can be confused with another (UserId vs OrderId vs SessionId), brand it: `type UserId = string & { readonly __brand: 'UserId' }`. Add a factory function that performs the only allowed cast.
rule.rationale: Without branding, getUser(orderId) compiles. Branding fails the compile at the call site rather than at the data layer. Factory functions concentrate the unsafe cast in one auditable place.

rule.statement: Narrow `unknown` with type-predicate functions (`value is T`) or Zod parse, never with `as T` casts. The only acceptable cast is the factory inside a branded-ID helper.
rule.rationale: `as T` is unchecked: invalid shapes pass at compile time and crash at runtime. Type predicates put the narrowing logic in code that can be unit-tested.

rule.statement: When the caller guarantees a value exists, use an assertion function (`asserts value is T`) that throws with a message. The non-null assertion operator (`!`) is forbidden on values that can legitimately be null/undefined.
rule.rationale: Assertion functions document intent ("I require this; here is why") and produce traceable error messages. `!` is invisible at runtime and shifts the failure to a meaningless TypeError downstream.

rule.statement: Function signatures crossing a public API or a module boundary MUST NOT contain `any`. Use `unknown` for unverified input and narrow before use. Internal helpers may use `any` only inside private utility code, never on exported signatures.
rule.rationale: `any` propagates through call sites and disables every downstream check. `unknown` forces explicit narrowing at the consumer, keeping safety local.
